attributes.stem:
A 17-month-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parent with a new rash that appeared this morning. He had been irritable with an increase in rhinrrhoea and fever earlier in the week but this has resolved by day three and he is back to his usual self. His older siblings were well but other children at his daycare had similar symptoms. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He has not had any vaccinations due to his parents' beliefs.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a blanching, rose-pink maculopapular rash on his trunk and neck, sparing his face. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This child presents with the classic biphasic pattern of roseola (also known as exanthem subitum or sixth disease), caused by Human herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6) and less commonly human herpesvirus 7 (HHV-7). The characteristic sequence is high fever for 3-5 days with malaise and rhinrrohoea, followed by rapid defervescence and appearance of a 2- 5 mm blanching rose-pink maculopapular rash when the child is clinically improving. Management is supportive only.

Roseola typically affects children between 6 months and 2 years of age. It is a common viral infection worldwide with up to 100% being seropositive by age three years. His siblings likely already have been exposed to roseola and thus would not demonstrate any symptoms. The rash is rose-pink, blanching, and maculopapular, primarily affecting the trunk and neck, sparing the face. It is usually non-pruritic and resolves within 1-2 days.

Measles presents with high fever, cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis (the '3 Cs') before the rash appears. The rash starts on the face and spreads downwards, appearing while the child is most unwell. Koplik's spots would be visible on the buccal mucosa. Whilst this child is unvaccinated so is at high risk, he lacks the other symptoms typically of measles.

Scarlet fever, caused by Streptococcus pyogenes, presents with fever, sore throat, and a characteristic 'sandpaper' rash which is not seen in this case. The rash typically starts in the axillae and groin, and the tongue becomes strawberry-red. There would be evidence of pharyngitis on examination.

Rubella typically presents with mild symptoms and a rash that starts on the face and spreads downwards. Posterior auricular and suboccipital lymphadenopathy are characteristic features, which are absent in this case.

Erythema infectiosum (fifth disease) typically presents with a 'slapped cheek' appearance followed by a lacy reticular rash on the trunk and limbs. The rash can fluctuate with temperature and stress, and there is usually minimal systemic upset. It is also a common viral illness however the clinical presentation is different in this case.
learningPoints: The rash of roseola appears when the child is improving clinically, unlike other viral exanthems.
Human herpesvirus 6 is the causative organism of roseola, primarily affecting children aged 6-24 months.
references: Pippin M, Laws G. A Classic Presentation of Roseola Infantum. Cureus. 2024 Jan 18;16(1). Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10874238/
Ngan V, Oakley A. Roseola. DermNet NZ. 2015. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/roseola
